title: Countries by income classification
subtitle: The World Bank's income classification divides countries into four categories based on their gross national income (GNI) per capita. Thresholds between income groups have changed over time.
caption:
Countries by income classification, World

The World Bank's income classification divides countries into four categories based on their gross national income (GNI) per capita. Thresholds between income groups have changed over time.

Between 1987 and 2024, the stacked area chart tracks the number of countries in each of the four World Bank income groups: low income, lower-middle income, upper-middle income, and high income. The vertical axis measures the count of countries, ranging from 0 to 250, while the horizontal axis spans the years 1987 through 2024. In 1987, the low-income group (purple) contained roughly 50 countries, the lower-middle income group (pink) held about 50, the upper-middle income group (light green) included approximately 30, and the high-income group (dark green) comprised around 40 countries. By 2024, the composition shifted: the low-income band narrowed to roughly 25 countries, the lower-middle income band contracted to about 55, the upper-middle income band expanded to approximately 55, and the high-income band grew to around 85 countries. The total number of countries classified rose from about 170 in 1987 to over 220 in 2024. The high-income category shows steady growth across the period, while the low-income category declines after peaking near 65 countries around 1995. The lower-middle income group fluctuates between 50 and 65 countries, and the upper-middle income group rises from 30 to 55. Countries are grouped based on the income classification for each respective year, meaning group membership can change over time. The most visible pattern is the expansion of the high-income tier alongside the contraction of the low-income tier over the 37-year span.
